questionText: When calculating dwelling unit loads using the optional method (220.82), what percentage is applied to the first 10 kVA of general loads?
explanation: NEC 220.82(B) specifies 100% of the first 10 kVA when using the optional calculation method for dwelling units. Loads over 10 kVA use 40%.
sparkyTip: Optional method: 100% for first 10kVA, then 40% for the rest. Simple two-tier calculation!

questionText: When using the Standard Calculation Method for a dwelling unit with fixed electric space heating rated at 10 kW and central air conditioning rated at 5 kW, what is the calculated load for the HVAC equipment?
explanation: Step 1: Compare the loads. Per NEC 220.60 (Noncoincident Loads), because heating and cooling do not run simultaneously, you only calculate the larger of the two (10 kW Heat vs 5 kW AC). Drop the 5 kW AC. Step 2: Apply the Standard Method demand factor. Per NEC 220.51, fixed electric space heating must be calculated at 100% of the total connected load. Therefore, the load used is 10 kW.
sparkyTip: Did you pick 6.5 kW? You just fell for the Optional Method trap! The 65% demand factor for heating ONLY applies when using the Optional Method (220.82). For the Standard Method, heat is always calculated at a full 100%.

questionText: A dwelling unit has 2,450 sq ft of floor area, 3 small-appliance branch circuits, and 1 laundry circuit. Using the Standard Method, what is the calculated general lighting and receptacle demand load in VA?
explanation: Step 1: Total connected load. Lighting: 2450 sq ft × 3 VA = 7350 VA (220.41). Small Appliance: 3 × 1500 = 4500 VA. Laundry: 1 × 1500 = 1500 VA. Total = 13350 VA. Step 2: Apply Table 220.42 demand factors. The first 3000 VA is calculated at 100% (3000 VA). The remainder (13350 - 3000 = 10350 VA) is calculated at 35% (10350 × 0.35 = 3622.5 VA). Step 3: Add them together. 3000 + 3622.5 = 6622.5 VA (rounds to 6623 VA).
sparkyTip: Beware the Frankenstein rule! Never mix 10,000 VA with 35%. For the Standard Method general load, ALWAYS use the first 3,000 VA at 100%, and the rest at 35%. I left the 'Frankenstein' math (11172 VA) in the options as a trap!

questionText: A feeder supplies three motors with full-load currents of 28 A, 18 A, and 12 A. Per 430.24, what is the minimum feeder conductor ampacity?
explanation: Feeder ampacity = 125% of largest (28 A) + 100% of others = 65 A.
sparkyTip: Largest motor at 125%, all others at 100%.

questionText: A continuous-duty motor has a full-load current (FLC) of 25 A. Using an inverse time circuit breaker sized at 250% per Table 430.52, what is the maximum standard OCPD rating permitted?
explanation: Step 1: Calculate the maximum rating. 25 A × 2.50 = 62.5 A. Step 2: Apply the exception. Per 430.52(C)(1) Exception No. 1, because 62.5 A is not a standard size, you are permitted to go up to the next standard size overcurrent device. Step 3: Find the standard size. Per 240.6(A), the next standard size above 60 A is 70 A.
sparkyTip: Motors break all the rules! A 62.5A calculation means you get to jump up to a 70A breaker. Test writers will always put the exact math answer (62.5A) and the 'round down' answer (60A) in the options to trick you. Always check your standard sizes in 240.6(A)!

questionText: A 120 V single-phase circuit carries 35 A and runs 120 ft one-way. The conductor resistance is 0.321 ohms per 1000 ft. What is the approximate voltage drop in volts?
explanation: Voltage drop = 2 x I x R x D / 1000 = 2.7 V.
sparkyTip: Use 2 x distance for single-phase voltage drop.

questionText: A 3-phase feeder carries 80 A and runs 225 ft one-way. The conductor resistance is 0.197 ohms per 1000 ft. What is the approximate line-to-line voltage drop in volts?
explanation: Voltage drop = 1.732 x I x R x D / 1000 = 6.14 V.
sparkyTip: 3-phase voltage drop uses 1.732 x I x R x D / 1000.

questionText: A 48-amp continuous load (such as an EV charger) requires a 60-amp overcurrent protective device. If wiring this installation using NM-B cable (Romex), what is the minimum size copper conductor required?
explanation: EV charging is a continuous load, requiring the circuit to be sized at 125% of the load (48A x 1.25 = 60A). Per NEC 334.80, NM-B cable must be sized using the 60°C column of Table 310.16. Since #6 NM-B is only rated for 55A, you must step up to #4 NM-B, which is rated for 70A.
sparkyTip: Watch out for NM cable! Even though the wire inside is 90°C, the Code forces you to use the 60°C column. This is why #6 THHN is okay for 60A, but #6 Romex is NOT.

questionText: An EV charger drawing a 48-amp continuous load is located 150 feet from the source. Using THHN copper in conduit, what is the voltage drop percentage, and does it meet the NEC recommendation for branch circuits?
explanation: Step 1: Use the formula VD = (2 x L x R x I) / 1000. (2 x 150ft x 0.491 x 48A) / 1000 = 7.07V. Step 2: 7.07V / 240V = 2.95%. NEC Informational Notes in 210.19(A) recommend a maximum 3% drop for branch circuits, so this installation is compliant but at the limit.
sparkyTip: At 150 feet, you're flirting with the 3% limit. If you have to go to 200 feet, you better pull #4 AWG to keep the charger happy and efficient!

questionText: A 48-amp EV charging station is being installed 80 feet away from the service panel using THHN copper conductors in PVC conduit. What is the minimum size conductor required to satisfy ampacity requirements, and what is the estimated voltage drop at 240V using Chapter 9, Table 8?
explanation: Step 1: Ampacity. Per 625.41, EV chargers are continuous loads (48A × 1.25 = 60A). Per Table 310.16, #6 THHN copper is rated for 65A at 75°C, satisfying the 60A requirement. Step 2: Voltage Drop. Using Chapter 9, Table 8, #6 copper has a resistance of 0.491 ohms/1000ft. VD = (2 × 80ft × 0.491Ω × 48A) / 1000 = 3.77V. Percentage drop = 3.77V / 240V = 1.57% (rounds to 1.6%). This is well within the 3% recommended limit.
sparkyTip: The Romex Trap! If you ran NM-B (Romex) instead of THHN in conduit, you'd be stuck in the 60°C column. #6 NM-B is only good for 55A, which fails the 60A requirement. You'd be forced to buy expensive #4 NM-B. THHN in pipe saves the day!

questionText: You are sizing the supply conductors for a transformer-type arc welder with a nameplate primary current of 50 amperes and a duty cycle of 50%. What is the minimum required ampacity for the conductors supplying this welder?
explanation: First, identify the welder type. A 'transformer-type' welder is a Non-motor-generator welder. Refer to Table 630.11(A) under the column 'Non-motor-generator'. For a 50% Duty Cycle, the multiplier is 0.71. Multiply the nameplate rating by this factor: 50A x 0.71 = 35.5A.
sparkyTip: Transformer-type = non-motor-generator. Find the duty cycle in Table 630.11(A), grab the multiplier, and multiply by nameplate amps. For 50% duty cycle, that's 0.71.

questionText: A restaurant kitchen is supplied by a 208Y/120-volt, 3-phase, 4-wire feeder. The kitchen contains the following electric equipment: one 10 kW oven, one 12 kW fryer, one 8 kW steamer, and one 9 kW booster heater. What is the line current for the feeder demand load?
explanation: Step 1: Total connected load = 10 + 12 + 8 + 9 = 39 kW. Step 2: Apply demand factor per Table 220.56 — for 4 units of kitchen equipment, the demand factor is 80%. 39 kW × 0.80 = 31.2 kW. Step 3: Calculate 3-phase amperage: I = W ÷ (V × √3) = 31,200 ÷ (208 × 1.732) = 31,200 ÷ 360.25 = 86.6A ≈ 87A.
sparkyTip: Table 220.56 demand factors by equipment count: 1-2 = 100%, 3 = 90%, 4 = 80%, 5 = 70%, 6+ = 65%. Count the pieces of equipment first, then multiply the total connected load by the factor.

questionText: An existing dwelling is fed by a 200A, 120/240V service. The homeowner is adding a detached residential shop and new electric space heating. The existing house has 26,000 VA of connected general lighting, receptacles, and appliances, plus a 5,000 VA AC unit. The new shop adds 7,200 VA of general lighting, a 5 HP 240V compressor (28A), and 10,000 VA of electric space heating. Using the Optional Method for an existing dwelling (220.83), does the 200A service have the capacity to handle this addition, and what is the new calculated load?
options: Yes, the total load is 128A, leaving 72A of spare capacity.
Yes, the total load is 133A, leaving 67A of spare capacity.
Yes, the total load is 149A, leaving 51A of spare capacity.
No, the total load is 208A, exceeding the service by 8A.
explanation: Step 1: Find total connected 'other' loads. House (26,000 VA) + Shop Lighting (7,200 VA) + Compressor (28A × 240V = 6,720 VA) = 39,920 VA. Step 2: Apply 220.83(B) demand. The first 8 kVA is at 100% (8,000 VA). The remainder (31,920 VA) is at 40% (12,768 VA). Total general = 20,768 VA. Step 3: Compare HVAC. Shop Heat (10,000 VA) is larger than House AC (5,000 VA), so keep 10,000 VA at 100%. Step 4: Total Load = 20,768 VA + 10,000 VA = 30,768 VA. Step 5: Convert to Amps. 30,768 VA / 240V = 128.2A. The 200A service handles it easily!
sparkyTip: Watch out for the traps! When using the Optional Method for an EXISTING dwelling addition (220.83), your magic number is 8 kVA at 100%, not 10 kVA. Also, unlike other calculations, you do not add an extra 25% to the largest motor here. Just use the straight FLC and drop it in the pot!

questionText: Using the Optional Method, calculate the total demand for an existing 1,800 sq. ft. dwelling unit with a 12 kW range, a 5 kW dryer, and two 20A small appliance branch circuits. The owner is adding a detached 2,400 sq. ft. residential shop fed from the same service, which contains a 10 kW electric heater and a 5 HP 240V compressor (28A). The home has a 5-ton AC unit (rated 30A at 240V). What is the total calculated load in Amps?
explanation: Step 1: General load = (1,800 + 2,400) sq ft × 3 VA = 12,600 VA. Step 2: Required circuits = (2 small appliance + 1 laundry *implicit requirement*) × 1,500 VA = 4,500 VA. Step 3: Appliances = 12,000 (range) + 5,000 (dryer) + 6,720 (motor: 28A × 240V) = 23,720 VA. Step 4: Total general = 12,600 + 4,500 + 23,720 = 40,820 VA. Step 5: Apply 220.82(B) demand — first 10,000 VA @ 100% = 10,000 + remainder 30,820 @ 40% = 12,328 → total = 22,328 VA. Step 6: HVAC per 220.82(C) — heating 10,000 @ 65% = 6,500 VA vs AC 30A × 240V = 7,200 VA @ 100% → use larger = 7,200 VA. Step 7: Final = 22,328 + 7,200 = 29,528 VA ÷ 240V = 123A.
sparkyTip: Did you catch the trap? The question didn't mention a laundry circuit, but the NEC requires one (1,500 VA) for dwelling units! Also, remember the Optional Method HVAC rule: compare the heat (at 65%) to the AC (at 100%) and only keep the larger of the two.

questionText: A fabrication shop is installing a new feeder to supply five nonmotor generator arc welders. All five welders operate at a 50% duty cycle, but they have different primary nameplate current ratings: 100A, 80A, 60A, 50A, and 40A. According to NEC Article 630, what is the minimum required ampacity for the feeder conductors supplying this specific group of welders?
explanation:
Step 1: Apply Table 630.11(A). The multiplier for a nonmotor generator arc welder at a 50% duty cycle is 0.71. Calculate the effective ampacity for each welder: W1 (100A × 0.71 = 71A), W2 (80A × 0.71 = 56.8A), W3 (60A × 0.71 = 42.6A), W4 (50A × 0.71 = 35.5A), W5 (40A × 0.71 = 28.4A). 

Step 2: Apply 630.11(B) Group Demand Factors. Stack them from largest to smallest and multiply: 
1st Largest (71A × 1.0) = 71A 
2nd Largest (56.8A × 1.0) = 56.8A 
3rd Largest (42.6A × 0.85) = 36.21A 
4th Largest (35.5A × 0.70) = 24.85A 
5th Largest (28.4A × 0.60) = 17.04A. 

Step 3: Add them up. 71 + 56.8 + 36.21 + 24.85 + 17.04 = 205.9 Amps (rounds to 206A).
sparkyTip: Order of operations is everything! You MUST shrink the individual welders based on their duty cycle FIRST. Then you take those new, smaller numbers, stack them from largest to smallest, and apply the 100/100/85/70/60% group discounts. If you do it backwards, your math falls apart!
